transcript: became the foundation of 100 Years of Solitude's style. Their small house in Aracataca, where the author spent his childhood, formed the main inspiration for Macondo. With 100 Years of Solitude, Gabriel Garcia Marquez found a unique way to capture the unique history of Latin America. He was able to depict the strange reality of living in a post-colonial society forced to relive the tragedies of the past. In spite of all this fatalism, the novel still holds hope. At his Nobel lecture, Garcia Marquez reflected on Latin America's long history of civil strife and rampant iniquity. Yet he ended the speech by affirming the possibility of building a better world, to quote, where no one will be able to decide for others how they die, where love will prove true and happiness be possible, and where the races condemned to 100 Years of Solitude will have, at last, forever, a second chance on Earth.
